emerging_technologies.name: Progressive Profiling for Consumer Identity
emerging_technologies.description: Progressive profiling is a consumer identity and access management (CIAM) strategy that collects user information incrementally over multiple interactions rather than requiring extensive registration data upfront. At first visit, the user may authenticate with just an email or social login. Over subsequent sessions, the application requests additional profile attributes (phone number, preferences, address) when contextually relevant, such as during checkout or when accessing premium features. This approach dramatically reduces registration abandonment rates (which can exceed 60% for lengthy forms) while eventually building a complete user profile. Progressive profiling respects user experience by only asking for information that is immediately needed, and it aligns with data minimization principles under GDPR and CCPA by collecting data with a clear, contemporaneous purpose. Modern CIAM platforms implement this through customizable authentication pipelines and post-login Actions or Rules.
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Standard practice in consumer-facing applications. Major CIAM platforms (Auth0, Okta CIC, ForgeRock, Ping) provide progressive profiling capabilities out of the box. E-commerce, media, and SaaS applications widely implement staged registration. Social login providers (Google, Apple, Facebook) enable minimal initial registration. The approach is driven by both UX optimization (higher conversion rates) and privacy regulation compliance (data minimization). Advanced implementations use machine learning to determine the optimal time and context to request additional profile attributes.
use_cases: E-commerce checkout with guest-to-registered user conversion
Media and content platforms with freemium-to-premium upgrade flow
Financial services with staged KYC from basic to full identity verification
Healthcare patient portals collecting clinical information as needed
SaaS trial-to-paid conversion with incremental organizational profile completion
emerging_technologies.migration_from: Monolithic registration forms, mandatory profile completion at signup, gated access requiring full profile

vendor_support.auth0: Actions and Rules pipeline for progressive profiling; Universal Login customization for staged attribute collection
vendor_support.okta: Okta Customer Identity Cloud (CIC) with progressive profiling hooks and user schema extension
vendor_support.forgerock: ForgeRock Identity Cloud with journey orchestration for staged profile completion
vendor_support.ping: PingOne DaVinci orchestration engine for conditional attribute collection flows
vendor_support.aws-cognito: Custom authentication challenges and Lambda triggers for progressive profiling

emerging_technologies.name: Privacy-First Consent Management
emerging_technologies.description: Privacy-first consent management integrates data consent collection, storage, and enforcement directly into the identity and access management layer. Rather than treating consent as a separate compliance checkbox, modern CIAM platforms make consent a core attribute of the user identity that gates data processing operations. When a user authenticates, their current consent state is evaluated and included in the access token or session context, enabling downstream services to automatically enforce consent-based data access policies. Consent management covers opt-in/opt-out preferences for marketing, data sharing with third parties, analytics tracking, and cross-border data transfer. The system maintains a complete audit trail of consent grants and withdrawals with timestamps, purposes, and the specific privacy notice version the user agreed to. Consent is presented as granular, purpose-specific choices rather than all-or-nothing acceptance.
standards: IAB Transparency and Consent Framework (TCF) v2.2
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Adoption accelerating due to regulatory pressure. GDPR enforcement actions (fines exceeding EUR 4 billion cumulative) have made consent management a board-level priority. California CPRA, Brazil LGPD, and India DPDPA are expanding consent requirements globally. OneTrust, Cookiebot, and TrustArc lead the consent management platform market, but these are often separate from the CIAM layer. Integration between CIAM and consent management is the current frontier, with vendors like Auth0, ForgeRock, and Ping building consent into the identity pipeline. The EU Digital Identity Wallet will include consent management for credential sharing.
use_cases: GDPR-compliant consent collection during user registration and login
Granular data sharing preferences for third-party integrations
Cookie consent management integrated with authentication state
Cross-border data transfer consent for multinational organizations
Consent-based API authorization restricting data access to consented purposes
emerging_technologies.migration_from: Terms-of-service checkbox at registration, cookie consent banners separate from identity, manual consent tracking in spreadsheets

vendor_support.onetrust: Consent management platform with CIAM integration through APIs and SDK
vendor_support.auth0: Custom consent flows through Actions pipeline; user metadata for consent state storage
vendor_support.forgerock: ForgeRock Identity Cloud with built-in consent management and privacy-by-design features
vendor_support.ping: PingOne with consent-aware authorization policies and data governance integration
vendor_support.criipto: Privacy-first identity verification with eIDAS integration and consent management

emerging_technologies.name: Gartner Identity Fabric Architecture
emerging_technologies.description: Identity Fabric is an architectural concept defined by Gartner that provides a unified, composable identity infrastructure spanning all identity use cases (workforce, consumer, machine, partner) across cloud, on-premises, and hybrid environments. Rather than deploying separate identity solutions for each use case (one IdP for employees, another for customers, manual processes for machine identities), Identity Fabric creates a cohesive layer that provides consistent identity services regardless of the consumer, protocol, or deployment model. The fabric exposes identity capabilities (authentication, authorization, lifecycle management, governance) as modular, API-driven services that can be composed into workflows for any use case. Identity Fabric acts as an abstraction layer over heterogeneous identity infrastructure, enabling organizations to migrate between vendors, adopt new protocols, and support new use cases without rebuilding their identity architecture.
standards: No formal standard; Gartner-defined architectural concept
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Early concept adoption by large enterprises. Gartner positions Identity Fabric as a strategic planning concept rather than a product category. Some vendors (Ping Identity, ForgeRock, SailPoint) are repositioning their platforms as Identity Fabric implementations. Most organizations are pursuing Identity Fabric incrementally by consolidating identity providers, implementing identity orchestration, and establishing API-driven identity services. True Identity Fabric implementations that span all identity types with a unified governance model remain rare and typically exist only in the largest enterprises with dedicated identity architecture teams.
use_cases: Unifying workforce, customer, and partner identity under a single governance framework
Abstracting identity infrastructure to enable multi-vendor strategy without lock-in
Providing consistent identity services across cloud, on-premises, and edge environments
Supporting mergers and acquisitions with rapid identity integration across heterogeneous environments
Enabling identity-as-a-service internal platforms for application development teams
emerging_technologies.migration_from: Siloed identity infrastructure per use case, vendor-locked identity platforms, manual identity integration through custom code

vendor_support.ping: PingOne platform positioning as Identity Fabric with DaVinci orchestration and multi-tenant capabilities
vendor_support.forgerock: ForgeRock Identity Cloud as a unified platform for workforce, consumer, and IoT identity
vendor_support.sailpoint: SailPoint Identity Security Cloud for identity governance across all identity types
vendor_support.saviynt: Enterprise Identity Cloud spanning IGA, PAM, and application access in a unified platform
vendor_support.microsoft: Microsoft Entra as an identity fabric covering workforce (Entra ID), consumer (External ID), permissions, and verified ID

emerging_technologies.name: Identity Orchestration Engines
emerging_technologies.description: Identity orchestration engines provide visual, no-code or low-code workflow builders for composing complex identity journeys from modular identity service components. Instead of hardcoding authentication flows in application code, orchestration engines allow identity architects to define dynamic, context-aware identity journeys that combine multiple identity services (MFA providers, risk engines, identity verification, consent management, fraud detection) into coherent user experiences. Orchestration engines evaluate real-time context (device posture, user risk score, transaction value, geographic location) to dynamically adjust the identity journey, such as adding step-up authentication for high-risk scenarios or skipping verification for known trusted contexts. This decouples identity logic from application code, enabling rapid iteration on authentication flows without application redeployment. Orchestration is a core enabler of Identity Fabric architecture.
standards: OpenID Shared Signals Framework (for real-time risk signals)
SCIM 2.0 (for identity data operations within workflows)
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Growing rapidly as organizations recognize the need for dynamic, composable identity flows. Ping Identity DaVinci and Transmit Security are market leaders in dedicated orchestration. Auth0 Actions and Okta Workflows provide embedded orchestration within their CIAM platforms. Large enterprises are adopting orchestration to reduce identity engineering effort and enable business-driven identity changes. The approach aligns with the industry shift from monolithic identity platforms to composable identity architectures. Gartner predicts that by 2027, identity orchestration will be a core capability of all enterprise identity platforms.
use_cases: Dynamic step-up authentication based on real-time risk assessment
Multi-vendor identity journey combining best-of-breed authentication and verification services
Self-service identity flows (registration, recovery, profile update) without custom application code
Fraud prevention workflows integrating device fingerprinting, behavioral analysis, and identity verification
Regulatory compliance flows that adapt to the user's jurisdiction (GDPR, CCPA, PSD2)
emerging_technologies.migration_from: Hardcoded authentication flows in application code, static MFA policies applied uniformly, manual identity process workflows

vendor_support.ping: PingOne DaVinci visual orchestration engine with 150+ connectors to identity and security services
vendor_support.transmit: Transmit Security orchestration platform with embedded identity verification and fraud detection
vendor_support.auth0: Auth0 Actions pipeline for event-driven orchestration; Forms for visual flow building
vendor_support.okta: Okta Workflows for no-code identity process automation with 70+ connectors
vendor_support.strata: Maverics identity orchestration for abstracting and modernizing legacy identity infrastructure

emerging_technologies.name: ITDR (Identity Threat Detection and Response)
emerging_technologies.description: Identity Threat Detection and Response (ITDR) is a security discipline focused on detecting and responding to identity-based attacks that target the identity infrastructure itself. While traditional SIEM and UEBA monitor for suspicious user behavior, ITDR specifically monitors the identity plane: directory services, identity providers, federation endpoints, certificate authorities, and privileged access management systems. ITDR detects attacks such as Golden Ticket and Silver Ticket attacks on Active Directory, DCSync attacks extracting credential hashes, OAuth consent phishing, token theft and replay, identity provider misconfiguration exploitation, and federation trust manipulation. ITDR solutions combine continuous assessment of identity infrastructure security posture (misconfigurations, excessive privileges, stale accounts) with real-time detection of identity-specific attack techniques. Response capabilities include automatic account lockout, session revocation, and identity infrastructure isolation.
standards: MITRE ATT&CK (Credential Access, Persistence, Lateral Movement tactics)
OCSF (Open Cybersecurity Schema Framework) for identity events
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Rapidly growing market driven by the increase in identity-based attacks (80%+ of breaches involve compromised credentials per Verizon DBIR). Gartner has recognized ITDR as a top security trend. Microsoft Defender for Identity, CrowdStrike Identity Threat Protection, SentinelOne Singularity Identity, and Silverfort lead the market. Traditional AD security tools (Semperis, Quest) are repositioning as ITDR vendors. The integration of ITDR with XDR (Extended Detection and Response) platforms is creating unified security operations that correlate identity, endpoint, and network telemetry. Organizations are establishing dedicated identity security operations (IdentitySecOps) teams.
use_cases: Detection of Active Directory attacks (Golden Ticket, DCSync, DCShadow, Kerberoasting)
Real-time monitoring of identity provider configuration changes for tampering
OAuth and OIDC token theft detection through impossible token use patterns
Privileged access abuse detection correlating PAM sessions with identity anomalies
Post-breach identity infrastructure hardening and compromise assessment
emerging_technologies.migration_from: Generic SIEM rules for authentication events, periodic Active Directory security assessments, manual review of identity infrastructure configurations

vendor_support.microsoft: Defender for Identity monitoring AD and Entra ID with attack detection and investigation
vendor_support.crowdstrike: Falcon Identity Threat Protection with AD security posture and real-time attack detection
vendor_support.sentinelone: Singularity Identity for AD attack surface reduction and identity threat detection
vendor_support.silverfort: Unified identity protection platform with MFA enforcement for legacy systems and ITDR
vendor_support.semperis: Active Directory security and recovery with Directory Services Protector for ITDR

emerging_technologies.name: Decentralized Authorization (Cedar, OPA, Zanzibar)
emerging_technologies.description: Decentralized authorization moves authorization decision-making from centralized policy servers to distributed policy engines running alongside or within applications, enabling microsecond-latency authorization decisions at scale. Unlike traditional centralized RBAC where a central server evaluates every access request, decentralized authorization distributes policy evaluation to edge locations while maintaining centralized policy management and auditing. Three major approaches have emerged: AWS Cedar provides a formal verification language for permission policies with provable correctness guarantees; Open Policy Agent (OPA/Rego) provides a general-purpose policy engine used across Kubernetes, API gateways, and applications; and Google Zanzibar (implemented by SpiceDB, Authzed, Ory Keto) provides a relationship-based access control (ReBAC) model that scales to trillions of authorization checks per second. These systems separate authorization logic from application code, making policies auditable, testable, and independently deployable.
emerging_technologies.adoption_status: Growing rapidly in cloud-native and microservices environments. OPA is a CNCF graduated project widely adopted for Kubernetes admission control, API authorization, and infrastructure policy. AWS Cedar powers Amazon Verified Permissions and is open-source. Google Zanzibar-inspired systems (SpiceDB, Authzed, OpenFGA) are gaining traction for applications requiring relationship-based access control at scale. The trend is away from embedded authorization logic (if/else in code) toward externalized, declarative policy languages. Major cloud providers now offer managed authorization services (AWS Verified Permissions, Google IAP, Azure RBAC with custom roles).
use_cases: Fine-grained API authorization in microservices without hardcoded permission checks
Kubernetes admission control and resource policy enforcement with OPA/Gatekeeper
Document and file sharing permission models (Google Docs-style) using Zanzibar/ReBAC
Multi-tenant SaaS authorization with tenant isolation and delegated administration
Compliance policy enforcement with auditable, version-controlled authorization rules
emerging_technologies.migration_from: Hardcoded if/else authorization in application code, monolithic RBAC tables in databases, centralized XACML policy decision points with high latency

vendor_support.aws: Amazon Verified Permissions using Cedar for application authorization; open-source Cedar SDK
vendor_support.styra: Styra DAS for enterprise OPA management with policy distribution and decision logging
vendor_support.authzed: SpiceDB open-source Zanzibar implementation; Authzed managed ReBAC service
vendor_support.openfga: CNCF Sandbox project for Zanzibar-style relationship-based authorization (originally Auth0/Okta)
vendor_support.ory: Ory Keto open-source access control server implementing Google Zanzibar concepts
